posts.name: Dan Teng
posts.cooked:
How’s 2016 in general?

Right when I’m starting to taste some 2015, 2016 are showing up!!
Wtf.

I know 2014 was great, and 2015 on the ripe side but admit I’ve enjoyed some 2015 recently.

I must have spaced out on reading anything about 2016.
What’s the reputation, vintage personality?

I’d look if we had a search function.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Tiny, complex crop that’s highly variable. There should be plenty of gems to be had.

posts.name: Eric Tomasi
posts.cooked:
expensive.

I may buy a tiny bit. So far all I bought was some from Maison Harbour to support them and keep the allocation. The prices are eye-watering and I am seeing comments from the chattering class that are reminding me of weaker years where I was burned. Unless you need to keep an allocation, you may want to wait until you taste a few.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Dan, we were there in early November, and tasted at three domaines (Bouley, Faiveley, Bruno Clair). The 16s were very good to excellent.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
dteng:

How’s 2016 in general?

Right when I’m starting to taste some 2015, 2016 are showing up!!
Wtf.

I know 2014 was great, and 2015 on the ripe side but admit I’ve enjoyed some 2015 recently.

I must have spaced out on reading anything about 2016.
What’s the reputation, vintage personality?

I’d look if we had a search function.

2014 “great”?
I´d rather call it very good/excellent … (and earlier accessable)

2016 (red): tiny crop (with no crop at all in some lieu-dits), much less consistent than 2015, but the top wines can be really outstanding …
less overall ripeness than 2015, the acidity usually showes more, the tannins less sweet, the best wines are transparent and nicely balanced, with enough depth and intensity …
but there are also wines lacking mid-weight (with a certain hollowness) … the taste is drier than 2015, which some might prefer … could turn out like some (outstanding) 1991s … (which also wasn´t very consistent) …
On the lower level I would taste before buying blind … but the best producers made fine wines …

(unfortunately prices did not fall, rather the opposite …)
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Arnt Egil Nordlien
posts.cooked: Tasted some hundreds, mostly at top level. It is definitely a variable vintage and it is not easy to categorize. Quality vary more on producer level, than geographically. Frost, mildew and hailproblems is the cause. The problems was early in the growing cycle, meaning a lot of grapes got loads of concentration. So much it was hard to cope with. Lots of wine over the top. Those that have been lucky with the conditions and have managed the vintage have made truly magnificent wines. The best 2016s are better than the best 2015s.

posts.cooked:
dteng:

How’s 2016 in general?

Right when I’m starting to taste some 2015, 2016 are showing up!!
Wtf.

I know 2014 was great, and 2015 on the ripe side but admit I’ve enjoyed some 2015 recently.

I must have spaced out on reading anything about 2016.
What’s the reputation, vintage personality?

I’d look if we had a search function.

From what I have tasted, 2015 is going to be a great vintage for top producers. The ones I have tasted have the structure to go with the fruit.

posts.name: Jo W Harbitz
posts.cooked:
2016 – simply unfair! Massive differences between different places, hail as well as frost. It’s an extremely small harvest, but also those not affected had a very good volume result too.

2016 will be the vintage for the experts. You will need to know the details on how the village and wine yard was affected by the conditions as well as the conditions around harvest time.

Good luck in other words.

But on a lighter note. If you feel 2014 is a great vintage (for reds) then you can handle everything 2016 throws your way

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Arnt Egil Nordlien:

Tasted some hundreds, mostly at top level. It is definitely a variable vintage and it is not easy to categorize. Quality vary more on producer level, than geographically. Frost, mildew and hailproblems is the cause. The problems was early in the growing cycle, meaning a lot of grapes got loads of concentration. So much it was hard to cope with. Lots of wine over the top. Those that have been lucky with the conditions and have managed the vintage have made truly magnificent wines. The best 2016s are better than the best 2015s.

While some 2016s, including some quite grand wines, are better than their 2015 counterparts, I don’t think that’s true in general. It would be hard to say that the 2016 La Tâche, for example, is superior to the 2015.

posts.name: Arnt Egil Nordlien
posts.cooked:
Dale Bowers:

I see you are in the fine wine auction business. You probably should have an ITB notation with your name or signature line.

Auctions are not my daily work, but I am professionally responsible for all wine auctions in Norway. My daily work is Senior Product Manager for Fine Wine at Vinmonopolet (norwegian wine monopoly). I will see what I can do. The signature would not save.

posts.name: Arnt Egil Nordlien
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

While some 2016s, including some quite grand wines, are better than their 2015 counterparts, I don’t think that’s true in general. It would be hard to say that the 2016 La Tâche, for example, is superior to the 2015.

I don’t disagree with your general view on 2016. But for DRC, I think 2016 is the most impressive wines I have tasted here from barrel. But I admit, I unfortunately never did taste the 2015s.
